I jumped over Spuds [stuffed dog doll] and I fell. About one-tenth of Earth's dry land and one-eighth of its oceans are covered with ice. This ice is made of snow that collects and becomes compacted (pressed down). Most ice occurs in thick ICE SHEETS that cap the land in the polar regions. In the past, during long cold eras called Ice Ages, ice covered much more of the Earth's surface than it does today. Scientists estimate that there have been over 15 Ice Ages in the last 2 million years. Ninety per cent of the world's ice is found in Antarctica. The ice cap here is 4,200 m (13,000 ft) deep in places. Over thousands of years, a thick ice sheet builds up over land when more snow falls during the winter months than melts each summer. The enormous weight of the ice pushes much of this vast, high landmass down below sea level. Icebergs are not formed from salty sea ice, but from land ice that calves (breaks off) from ice sheets or glaciers on the coast. Only 12 per cent of the iceberg's mass appears above the sea surface. The rest is hidden below. A fringe of sea ice also edges the Antarctic landmass, expanding in winter and melting in summer. Glaciers are slow-moving rivers of ice that begin high on mountains. Fallen snow pressed down by new snow forms a dense ice called firn. When enough ice builds up, gravity and the glacier's own weight set it sliding downhill at a rate of 1-2 m (3-6 1/2 ft) per day. Moving ice is a powerful erosive force. As glaciers slip downhill they carve deep, U-shaped valleys, sharp peaks, and steep ridges. The gouging power of the ice is increased by rocks and boulders carried along at the front, sides, and beneath the glacier. When the glacier reaches the warmer lowlands, it melts. They found out that both ideas were right - light was somehow both waves and particles. The Double-slit experiment performed by Thomas Young showed that light must act like a wave. The Photoelectric effect discovered by Albert Einstein proved that light had to act like particles that carried specific amounts of energy, and that the energies were linked to their frequencies. This experimental result is called the "wave-particle duality" in quantum mechanics. Later, physicists found out that everything behaves both like a wave and like a particle, not just light. Booting into Recovery Mode on a Samsung Galaxy: A Comprehensive Guide ================================================================== Introduction ------------ Recovery mode is a diagnostic state that allows you to perform various functions on your Android device without having to fully boot up the operating system. This can be particularly useful when troubleshooting issues or performing maintenance tasks such as wiping data, installing updates, or creating backups. In this article, we will walk you through the steps required to successfully boot your Samsung Galaxy smartphone or tablet into recovery mode. ### Key Tips and Guidelines * Be aware that booting into recovery mode may erase all data on your device if you choose to factory reset it. Always ensure important files are backed up before proceeding with these steps. * Make sure your battery level is above 50% to prevent any potential interruptions during the process due to low power. * The exact button combination needed to enter recovery mode varies between different models of Samsung devices. We have provided general guidance below; however, you should consult your specific user manual or online resources for your particular model to confirm which buttons to press. Steps to Boot into Recovery Mode --------------------------------- 1. **Turn off your device.** Press and hold the Power button until the 'Power Off' option appears on the screen. Tap on it and wait for your phone to turn off completely. It might take several seconds for this to happen. Why? Turning off your device ensures that there won't be any conflicts while trying to access the recovery partition. 2. **Enter Download Mode (also known as Odin Mode).** To do so, simultaneously press and hold down three keys - Volume Down + Home Button (+ Power Button if applicable) - depending upon your device model. Refer to your user manual to verify which combination applies to your specific Samsung device. Continue holding these buttons even after the manufacturer logo appears on the screen. !!! warning "Caution" Take care not to confuse Download Mode with Emergency Call Mode. If you accidentally activate Emergency Call Mode instead, simply hit the Back button to exit. 3. **Navigate to Recovery Mode using volume keys.** Once in Download Mode, release all buttons. Now press the Volume Up button once to highlight 'Recovery Mode'. You cannot navigate further using touch input at this stage; only the volume keys work. 4. **Confirm selection by pressing Power Button.** After selecting 'Recovery Mode', press the Power Button to confirm your choice and initiate the transition from Download Mode to Recovery Mode. Your display should now show an android robot lying on its back with a red exclamation mark over it. 5. **Access Text Menu in Recovery Mode.** While still viewing the android robot image, quickly tap the Volume Up button followed by the Power Button together. This action should bring up a text menu containing options like Reboot System Now, Apply Update From ADB, Factory Reset / Wipe Data, etc. You have now successfully entered Recovery Mode! At this point, feel free to explore available options according to your needs. Remember, though, that choosing certain options could potentially delete personal data stored on your device, so exercise caution and make informed decisions. Conclusion ---------- By following our guide, you should now know exactly how to boot your Samsung Galaxy device into Recovery Mode safely and efficiently. Whether you need to wipe data, flash custom ROMs, create Nandroid backups, or execute other advanced operations, familiarizing yourself with this procedure opens up new possibilities for managing and maintaining your Android device. Happy tweaking! I think I'll talk to your mom. Weighing BricksMath brain teasers require computations to solve. A bricklayer has 9 bricks. 8 bricks weigh the same amount, but one brick is slightly heavier than the rest. The bricklayer wants to find the heavy brick. He has a balance scale. How can he find the heavy brick in only two weighings? AnswerHe should divide the bricks into 3 groups of 3. He then weighs 2 sets of 3 bricks against each other. If they balance, the heavy brick is in the group that was set aside. If they do not balance, he then takes the group that was heaviest. He has 3 bricks left. He takes the 2 bricks from the 3 and weighs them against each other. If they balance, the heavy brick is the one which is set aside. If they do not balance, the scale will show which is the heavier brick. See another brain teaser just like this one... Lesson 1-The Scale In writing music there are many things to study, as counterpoint, theory, harmony, and rhythm. In the past, when most of our hymns were written, these subjects were more common. In the next few lessons we will study what the singing schools of our country taught about writing hymns. In this first lesson we will cover what scales and intervals are. A scale is a series of eight tones made up of certain intervals. An interval is the difference in pitch between tones. We will look at the C scale to discover a major scale and two kinds of intervals. The C major scale covers from C to C on the piano. It has eight tones and is called a major scale, because it has a series of whole and half steps in a certain order. First, we must discover what are whole and half steps. We can do this by looking at the piano keys. Below are the keys from C to C. From C to D is one whole step, because there is a black key between C and D. From E to F is a half-step because there is no note between E and F. If there is any black or white key between two notes, the interval is a whole step. If there are no keys between two notes, the interval is a half-step. Now, we call from C to C on the piano a major scale, because there is a pattern of whole, whole, half, whole, whole, whole, half steps. This pattern is in all the major scales. On the piano keys below find the pattern of w, w, h, w, w, w, h intervals. Try finding the same pattern of intervals in the G major scale. It is a series of eight tones starting at G and ending at G. Note the G major scale has an F# note in it to make it a major scale. Find the same pattern of steps in the D major scale. Note that there is an F# and C# to make it a major scale. In English, a simple sentence with a verb (an action), subject (who or what is doing the action), and an object (to whom or what the action is done) is written with a subject-verb-object word order (SVO). For example, in the sentence "Robert opens the door", "Robert" is the subject, "opens" is the verb and "door" is the object. SVO is the second-most common word order among all languages and is used in 42% of them. Examples are Mandarin Chinese, Bahasa Melayu, Bahasa Indonesia, Spanish, French, Italian, Thai and Vietnamese. While some of the above languages can use other word orders, such as SOV and VSO, they use SVO for the simplest sentences. Palliative care focuses on basic decisions people make when they are very close to the end of their lives, and it ensures someone is always available to talk to them. It is a replacement for heroic medical intervention that may keep them physically alive but with no quality of life. Catching shad with a throw net during winter can be a challenging but rewarding experience. This article will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to catch shad using a throw net in the winter season. Step 1: Understand the Basics of Shad Fish Shad is an essential forage fish that plays a critical role in the aquatic ecosystem. They are abundant in many rivers and tributaries throughout North America, especially in the southeastern states. During winter, shad migrate downstream to their spawning grounds, making them easier to target. Step 2: Choose the Right Type of Throw Net There are different types of throw nets available in the market, but the most suitable one for catching shad during winter is a circular or round net with small mesh size. A net with a diameter of around six feet and a mesh size of about inch would work perfectly well. The smaller mesh size ensures that even tiny shads do not escape while throwing the net. Key Tip: Always ensure your net is legal to use in your area before purchasing and using it. Some regions have restrictions on specific types of fishing gear. Step 3: Locate Schools of Shad To locate schools of shad, look for areas where there's slow-moving water near deep channels or holes. These locations offer shelter and food sources for shad. Also, observe any birds diving into the water as they often feed on baitfish like shad. Step 4: Prepare Your Equipment Aside from your throw net, gather other necessary equipment such as waders (if you plan to stand in the water), pliers to remove hooks safely, and buckets to store caught fish. It's also advisable to bring extra clothes, snacks, and drinks. Step 5: Practice Proper Technique When Casting the Net Stand at least ten feet away from the targeted school of shad. Hold onto the edge of the net with both hands, stretching it out flat in front of you. Swiftly toss the net over the school of shad, ensuring its edges land gently on the water surface so as not to scare off the fish. Once thrown, pull the ends together quickly, trapping the shad inside the net. Guideline: Remember, practice makes perfect. Don't get discouraged if you don't succeed initially; keep trying until you master the technique. Step 6: Retrieve Your Catch After successfully casting the net, retrieve your catch promptly by lifting the edges upward, gathering the bottom of the net, and shaking excess water off. Transfer the trapped shad into your bucket carefully. Step 7: Release Undesirable Catches Not all captured fishes may be desirable or allowed to keep. Familiarize yourself with local regulations regarding minimum length limits and daily bag limits. If unsure, release unwanted species back into the water immediately. Step 8: Store Your Gear Correctly Clean and dry your net thoroughly after every use to prevent mildew buildup. Store it in a cool, dark place protected from direct sunlight. Regular maintenance extends the lifespan of your gear. In conclusion, catching shad with a throw net during winter requires patience, skill, and knowledge about the behavior of these fish. By following this detailed tutorial, you increase your chances of having a successful shad fishing trip! He's right here, he's not goin' anywhere. An MLK Day with Meaning Patch has some great ideas for teaching your kids about diversity and acceptance this Martin Luther King Day, Jan. 21. Your child might think of Monday, Jan. 21 as just another day off from school. But you can use Martin Luther King Jr., Day as an opportunity to teach them about the civil rights movement, the importance of racial equality, Martin Luther King Jr., and his legacy. Celebrate Martin Luther King Jr., Day by talking to your children about King. What do they know about the man, and about the civil rights movement? What do they know about King's famous "I Have a Dream" speech? What do they believe was his intended message? Ask your children to tell you what they've learned about King, and then help fill in the gaps. (Many children think Dr. King was a phyician!) For example, do they know that he was the youngest man to receive the Nobel Peace Prize? If you need some help, click here. Learning about King and his hopes for the future is a wonderful way to celebrate and honor him. Other possible teachable moments: - Read King's "I Have a Dream" speech. Or, listen to King deliver the famous speech himself. - Volunteer. King was such a proponent of civil service, so one of the greatest ways to honor his legacy is to give back to your community. In fact, in recent years, growing numbers of Americans have spent Martin Luther King Jr., Day volunteering, in what has become known as the Martin Luther King Jr., Day of Service. Spend an afternoon helping out at a soup kitchen or homeless shelter. Visit with residents at a nursing home. Give a few hours at your local library or YMCA. If you need help finding a place to donate your time, enter your zip code at Volunteermatch.org or USAfreedomcorps.gov. - Watch the Presidential Inauguration for Barack Obama's second term, which will be held on Martin Luther King Jr., Day, in Washington D.C. Talk to your children about the importance of diversity and racial equality in our society. Could America's first black president have been elected if not for the efforts of Martin Luther King Jr.? Why or why not? - Be kind to others. King dreamed of a world in which his children would be "judged by the color of their skin but by the content of their character." Be kind and tolerant, and stand up to those who are not kind and tolerant of others. - Read about King in these books, which may be available at your local library: - A Picture Book of Martin Luther King, Jr. by by David A. Adler - My Brother Martin: A Sister Remembers Growing Up With the Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. by Christine King Farris - Dear Dr. King: Letters from Today's Children to Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. edited by Jan Colbert and Ann McMillan Harms In a statement on March 5, 2009, Eminem stated that he would release two albums that year. Title: How to Change Your Slack Username on PC or Mac Slack is a popular communication platform that allows you to collaborate with your team efficiently. If you want to change your Slack username due to personal preferences, this comprehensive guide will help you through the process on both PC and Mac devices. We'll provide clear steps along with explanations so that users at any level can confidently modify their usernames. **Step 1:** Open your preferred web browser (Google Chrome, Safari, Firefox, etc.) and navigate to <https://slack.com/> * This action takes you to the official Slack website where you can access your workspace and make necessary changes. By using a web browser instead of the desktop app, we ensure consistency across various platforms and easier troubleshooting if needed. **Step 2:** Log into your Slack account using your email address and password associated with your current workspace. * Correct login credentials are crucial as they determine whether you have permission to alter settings within your workspace. Make sure there are no typos while entering them. **Step 3:** Once logged in, click on the workspace name located at the top left corner of the screen. From the dropdown menu, select **Settings & administration**, then choose **Workspace settings**. * Accessing Workspace Settings grants us administrative privileges over our profile information, enabling us to perform tasks such as changing usernames. ![Accessing Workspace Settings](#) **Step 4:** On the left-hand sidebar under 'Settings', click on **Manage members**. A list displaying all workspaces members should appear. Find yourself in the list and click on your existing username. * Managing Members section gives an overview of every member involved in the workspace. Here, we locate ourselves amidst others to proceed with editing our profiles. ![Finding Yourself in the Member List](#) **Step 5:** Under Profile Information, locate the field labeled 'Name'. Enter your new desired username here. Remember, you cannot change your email address directly from these settings; only admins can do that via the Admin Dashboard. * The Name field represents what other users see when interacting with you on Slack. It doesn't affect your email address linked to your Slack account, but keep in mind character limitations and avoid special characters which might cause confusion. ![Changing Your Username](#) **Key Tips and Guidelines:** - Changing your username does NOT log you out from active sessions nor disrupts ongoing conversations. However, informing your teammates about the update may be helpful for continued smooth collaboration. - Note that certain integrations or bots connected to your previous username might stop functioning correctly until updated accordingly. Review any automated processes tied to your old username after making the switch. - Depending on your organization's policy, some restrictions could prevent you from changing your username. Reach out to your workspace administrator for assistance in such cases. By following these straightforward steps, you can successfully change your Slack username on either PC or Mac. Enjoy your newly customized identity and enhanced user experience! XXX flower and big and little flower. Right but we need ta read this story first. Today we're going to have some fun exploring interesting shapes and patterns using easy math concepts you learn in school. We will see three cool tricks involving circles, triangles, and graphs! Grab your pencils and paper, and let's dive in! **Trick #1:** Find the special point on a circle (Grades 6-7) Imagine drawing a circle with two points, B and C, on it. Your task is to find point A on the arc BC so that the sum of distances AB and AC is maximized. You might guess that A should be at one end of the arc-but actually, **A turns out to be the midpoint of the arc!** This neat trick shows us how sometimes the middle is the best place to be when trying to make something as big as possible. Can you draw other examples to verify this? **Trick #2:** Discover a hidden triangle pattern (Grades 4-5) Take any straight line and mark three points on it: A, B, and C. Now, connect these points to form a triangle ABC. Next, add another point D anywhere along side AB. Interestingly, if you measure the lengths AD and DC, their difference always equals the length of side DB! Isn't that strange and fascinating? By playing around with different lines and points, you can check whether this works every time. **Trick #3:** Draw like Spiderman with a Cobweb Plot (Grades 7-8) Let's create a spider web-like shape called a cobweb plot. First, pick a starting number, x (for example, x = 0.5). Next, draw a vertical line upwards until you hit the diagonal line y=f(x), where f(x) could be any simple function like f(x)=2x or f(x)=x/2. At the intersection, draw a horizontal line connecting back to the x-axis. Repeat this process using your newly found x-value as input. As you continue iterating, notice how the points seem to crawl towards a particular value! Curious, isn't it? Using various functions, experiment and discover what happens each time! These were just three of many exciting math surprises waiting to be discovered. Remember, mathematics is everywhere, even in places you least expect it. So keep asking questions and enjoy unraveling its secrets! Happy learning! If you eat up your hamburger you can have daddy's onion rings okay? Childproofing a door screen is an important task for parents and caregivers who want to ensure their children's safety at home. A door screen can pose a risk to young children because they may not be strong enough to open it from the inside, which could lead to them becoming trapped. By following this detailed tutorial, you will learn how to childproof your door screen effectively. **Things You'll Need:** 1. Screwdriver or drill 2. 3-inch (7.6 cm) screws 3. Two eye hooks 4. Chain lock or sliding bolt lock 5. Measuring tape **Step 1: Choose the Right Locking Mechanism** The first step in childproofing a door screen is selecting the appropriate locking mechanism. There are several options available, including chain locks, sliding bolt locks, and loop locks. When choosing a lock, consider its durability, ease of installation, and compatibility with your door screen. We recommend using a chain lock or sliding bolt lock as they provide a secure barrier while still allowing adults to exit quickly in case of emergency. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Make sure the lock is made of sturdy material that cannot be easily broken by a child. * Select a lock that requires minimal effort to operate so that it does not become frustrating for adults to use frequently. **Step 2: Measure the Distance Between the Door Frame and Screen** Before installing the chosen lock, measure the distance between the door frame and the screen. This measurement will help determine where to place the eye hooks and screws for optimal security and functionality. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Use a measuring tape to take accurate measurements. * Record the measurement for future reference during installation. **Step 3: Install Eye Hooks on Both Sides of the Door Frame** Using a screwdriver or drill, attach one eye hook to the top corner of the door frame and another eye hook to the bottom corner on the same side. These eye hooks will serve as anchors for the chain or sliding bolt lock. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Ensure that both eye hooks are installed at equal heights to maintain balance and prevent unnecessary strain on the door frame. * Tighten the screws securely but avoid overtightening, which may damage the door frame or weaken its structural integrity. **Step 4: Attach the Chain Lock or Sliding Bolt Lock** Connect the chain lock or sliding bolt lock to the two eye hooks installed earlier. The lock should span the distance between the door frame and screen without leaving any gaps through which a child could potentially escape. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Adjust the length of the chain or sliding bolt as needed to fit snugly against the screen when closed. * Test the lock by gently pulling on the screen to ensure it remains securely fastened. **Step 5: Secure the Other End of the Lock to the Door Screen** Attach the other end of the chain lock or sliding bolt lock to the door screen using screws. This connection point should be strong enough to withstand pressure from a child attempting to open the screen. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Drill pilot holes before inserting screws to make the process easier and reduce the likelihood of damaging the screen. * Double-check that all connections are tight and secure after installation. By following these steps, you have successfully childproofed your door screen, ensuring your child's safety while also maintaining easy access for adults. Regularly inspect the lock and its components to ensure they remain functional and secure over time. Mommy this fun to wrap around yourself too. It's like XXX I made the shield's operational again. Some chemical reactions need high energy to happen. An example is the breakdown of water into hydrogen and oxygen. An electrical cell (or an "electrolytic cell") is used for these reactions. It is a container which has to have a chemical reaction involving electrodes. The chemical substances are exposed to electrical power, and the electrolysis reaction happens inside the electrical cell. Title: Guess If a Coin Will Be Heads or Tails and Be Right Every Time Introduction: While it may seem impossible to guess the outcome of a coin flip every time, there is actually a trick that allows you to do so with impressive accuracy. By understanding the physics behind coin flips and employing some simple techniques, you can significantly increase your chances of correctly predicting whether a coin will land on heads or tails. This tutorial will guide you through the process, providing detailed explanations for each step along the way. Materials Required: * A coin (preferably one that is well-balanced) * A flat surface * Optional: Video recording equipment (such as a smartphone camera) to analyze the coin's spin Step 1: Understanding Coin Flip Physics When a coin is tossed into the air, its motion is determined by several factors including gravity, air resistance, initial velocity, and angular momentum. The coin rotates around an axis passing through its center of mass during flight. As the coin falls back towards the ground, it begins to lose both linear and angular momentum due to air resistance. At this point, the side facing upwards when the coin was at its peak height has a higher probability of landing face-up because it experiences less drag than the other side. Key Tip: Use a well-balanced coin to minimize any bias in the outcomes. Step 2: Observation and Timing Observe the coin closely as it is being tossed. Pay attention to which side is initially facing up when the toss begins. Ideally, you should attempt to call out your prediction just before the coin reaches its peak height. Guideline: Practice makes perfect - train yourself to recognize the crucial moment when you need to make your prediction. Step 3: Predicting the Outcome Based on your observation from Step 2, predict that the side facing upwards at the peak height will be the same side that lands face-up. Remember, this method relies on the fact that the upward-facing side generally experiences less drag, making it more likely to land face-up. Key Tip: Focus on calling out your predictions consistently at the right moment rather than trying to gauge the exact rotation pattern of the coin. Overthinking can lead to mistakes. Step 4: Analyzing Your Results Keep track of your success rate over multiple trials. While it might take some practice to master the technique, many people find they are able to improve their accuracy considerably compared to simply guessing without observing the coin's motion. Optional Step 5: Advanced Analysis Using Technology For those interested in further refining their predictions, consider using video recording equipment such as a smartphone camera to capture slow-motion footage of the coin flips. You can then study the recordings frame-by-frame to gain deeper insights into the relationship between the coin's orientation and its final resting position. Conclusion: Although no method guarantees a 100% success rate when guessing the outcome of a coin flip, understanding the underlying physics and applying careful observation can drastically enhance your ability to make accurate predictions. With consistent practice, anyone can learn this intriguing skill! Writing was invented independently a number of times. The Sumerian, the ancient Egyptian, the Chinese and the Mayan writings are separate in their invention. All these writing systems started with "pictographs", symbols that stood for things. Then they developed a mixture of methods. Our own alphabetic system is different. It is based on the sounds of spoken language. All alphabets are modified versions of the first one, which originated with the Phoenicians and the Ancient Greeks. What would you like to do first, Bobby? How many points are won or lost depends on the scoring system (not discussed here). Harold S. Vanderbilt played a key role in designing the scoring system, and in 1927 the Whist Club of New York produced a code for contract bridge. Title: How to Clean a Diskwasher Filter Dishes coming out of your dishwasher still dirty or smelly? It might be time to clean the dishwasher filter! Over time, food particles and debris can build up in the filter, preventing your dishes from getting properly cleaned. This tutorial will guide you through the process of cleaning a dishwasher filter with clear, easy-to-follow steps. **Things You'll Need:** 1. Dish soap or detergent 2. Soft brush (like an old toothbrush) 3. Hot water 4. Towel or rag 5. Distilled white vinegar (optional) 6. Baking soda (optional) 7. Toothpick or skewer (optional) ### **Step 1: Locate Your Dishwasher Filter** Before you start cleaning, locate the filter in your dishwasher. Most modern dishwashers have two components to their filters - an upper cylindrical screen and a lower flat mesh filter. These are typically found at the bottom of the dishwasher tub, near the drain. Consult your owner's manual if you need help finding yours. ![Locating the dishwasher filter](https://www.wikihow.com/images/thumb/a/aa/Clean-a-Dishwasher-Filter-Step-1-Version-2.jpg/aid1980534-v4- Yeah why, what was wrong with bright light? oh, it looks like there's a thread holding them together. Title: How to Build a Hobo Stove A hobo stove is a simple, portable wood-burning stove that can be made from recycled materials like metal cans or buckets. It's an excellent tool for camping, emergency preparedness, or just having fun with DIY projects. This article will provide you with a comprehensive guide on building your own hobo stove using a #10 can (a large food service can) as the primary material. **Materials Needed:** * A clean and dry #10 can (approximately 6 inches tall and 7 inches wide) * Sharp scissors or tin snips * Pliers * Marker pen * Ruler or measuring tape * Protective gloves and eyewear * Steel punch or nail setter * A small piece of wire mesh or expanded steel (at least 8x8 inches) **Step 1: Preparation** Put on protective gloves and eyewear before starting this project. Make sure the #10 can is thoroughly cleaned, dried, and free from sharp edges. Label one side of the can as the bottom using a marker pen. **Step 2: Creating the Fuel Chamber Openings** Measure about 3 inches upwards from the marked bottom edge and draw three equally spaced lines around the circumference of the can using a ruler and marker pen. These lines should divide the lower part of the can into four equal sections. Carefully make holes along these lines approximately 1 inch apart, ensuring they are centered horizontally. Use a hammer and a steel punch or nail setter to create the holes while wearing protective gloves and eye protection. The size of the holes depends on your preference; smaller ones may result in slower but more controlled burning, whereas larger holes allow for quicker combustion. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Be cautious when making the holes, ensuring you don't deform the can excessively, which could lead to instability during operation. * Space out the holes evenly for better fuel efficiency and heat distribution. **Step 3: Cutting Ventilation Holes** On the opposite side of the can, measure 1 inch downward from the rim and mark a continuous line all around the perimeter. Then, cut carefully along this line using sharp scissors or tin snips to remove the circular section. This opening serves as air intake ventilation for efficient burning. **Step 4: Forming the Pot Stand** From the wire mesh or expanded steel sheet, cut two pieces, each roughly 4x4 inches square. Bend both squares at their centers vertically so that they form a cross shape with legs that fit inside the diameter of the can without touching its sides. You now have a pot stand that allows air circulation underneath your cooking vessel. **Step 5: Assembling the Hobo Stove** Place the pot stand onto the open top portion of the can, ensuring there is enough space between the base of your cooking pot and the top surface of the pot stand for proper airflow. If necessary, adjust the length of the pot stand legs by bending them further until satisfied with the clearance. Congratulations! Your homemade hobo stove is ready to use. To operate, place it on level ground, gather some twigs, leaves, or other biomass as fuel, light the fuel chamber through the ventilation hole, and start cooking. Enjoy responsibly and safely! And they're all the animals and everything, and they walk around and feed them all. On Saturday, he ate through one place. Adding shows or movies to your watchlist on Hulu using an iPhone or iPad is an excellent way to keep track of content you want to view later. This tutorial will provide a step-by-step guide with clear explanations and helpful tips for adding titles to your Hulu watchlist on iOS devices. **Step 1: Launch the Hulu app on your iPhone or iPad** First, locate the Hulu app on your device's home screen or app library. Tap on the icon to open the application. If you haven't yet installed the Hulu app, download it from the App Store and sign up or log in to your account. **Step 2: Search for the title you wish to add to your watchlist** Tap on the magnifying glass icon at the bottom of the screen to access the search function. Type the name of the show or movie you are looking for in the search bar and hit 'Search.' Browse through the results until you find the correct title. **Step 3: Navigate to the details page of the title** Once you have found the title you want to add to your watchlist, tap on its thumbnail or title to go to its details page. The details page contains information about the cast, crew, synopsis, and episodes (for TV series). It also has options for watching, adding to watchlist, and sharing the title. **Step 4: Locate and tap the '+' button** On the top right corner of the details page, there should be a grey '+' button. This is the 'Add to' button that allows users to add titles to their watchlists. Tapping this button will immediately add the selected title to your Hulu watchlist. A confirmation message will appear briefly at the bottom of the screen stating that the title has been added to your watchlist successfully. *Key tip*: You can quickly access your watchlist by tapping the profile picture icon at the bottom right corner of the screen and selecting 'Watchlist' from the menu. **Step 5: Verify the addition of the title to your watchlist** To ensure that the title has indeed been added to your watchlist, navigate back to your profile page by tapping the profile picture icon at the bottom right corner of the screen and selecting 'Watchlist.' Scroll down to check if the newly added title appears in the list. *Guideline*: Remember that only logged-in users can add titles to their personalized watchlists. Make sure you are signed in to your Hulu account before attempting to add any titles. In conclusion, following these five steps will enable you to efficiently build your Hulu watchlist using your iPhone or iPad. By organizing your favorite shows and movies in one place, you can optimize your viewing experience and never miss out on great content again. Here put the screwdriver in here and we can turn the screws. This lesson is designed around a short film commissioned by John Lewis as their Christmas TV commercial. Students practice vocabulary related to journeys and Christmas, prepositions, song lyrics, speaking and writing. Language level: Intermediate (B1) - Advanced (C1) Learner type: Teens and adults Time: 60 minutes Activity: Watching short film, completing the lyrics of a song, speaking and writing Topic: Journeys and Christmas Language: Narrative tenses, prepositions and vocabulary related to Christmas Materials: Short film and song lyrics Write snowman on the board and ask students what comes to mind when they see the word. Put them in pairs and ask them to discuss the following questions: Have you ever built a snowman? Who did you build the snowman with? What did you use to give the snowman arms, eyes, a nose and a mouth? If you are teaching in a country where there isn't any snow ask the following questions: Have you seen a snowman in a film or cartoon? Would you like to build a snowman? What would you use to give the snowman arms, eyes, a nose and a mouth? Show your students the image of the snowman and snowwoman. Ask then the following question: Are they similar to the snowman or snowwoman you built as a child? Tell them that the image is taken from the start of a short film called The Journey. Show them the start until 00:15. Put students in pairs and ask them to write a story titled The Journey which explains what happens in the rest of the film. Give them 10 minutes to write their stories and help them with any vocabulary. Get one student from each group to read out their story and discuss each story. Now show the rest of the film until 1:21. Ask the students the following questions: What story does the film tell? Did you like the story? Did you like the film? How does the film make you feel? Does the film have a message? What does the film say about Christmas? Show the film until 1:23 and pause when you see the caption: "Give a little more love this Christmas." Ask your students what the caption means. Now show the rest of the film. Students will see that it is a commercial. Explain that John Lewis is a department store which is famous for its Christmas commercials. Ask them if they think the caption "Give a little more love this Christmas" has another meaning. Give feedback on the stories and correct any mistakes with the use of prepositions. Write up the following phrases: The snowman and snowwoman were standing next to each other. The snowman went across a field. The snowman climbed up a mountain. The snowman went down a mountain. The snowman went across a river. The snowman walked along a road. The snowman looked up at something. Tell your students that they are going to watch the film again; this time they should listen to the song which accompanies the film and say if they think it is a suitable song for the film. Give students the lyrics to the song which has all the preposition removed; tell them they should complete the lyrics with a partner. Play the film again and students check their answers. Ask students the following questions: Why do you think this song was chosen for the film? Does the song add anything to the film? How does the song make you feel? Tell students to write a narrative of the story told in the film, they should make their narrative as descriptive as possible and try to use some of the expression from the song lyrics. I hope you enjoy the film and the lesson. Where did it go oh I can't get them the part. Does it height be in pencil it said XXX in pencil. You going to see somethin' else you like? ?[exits then enters with a toy high+chair] First published in the MLA Newsletter, November, 2011 The MLA continues its series of articles on endangered whales and the laws in place to protect them. New whale rules to reduce the risk of entangling endangered whales in vertical lines will go into effect in 2014. The fifth part of this series investigates how right whales' vision could affect the gear fishermen use. Before comparing a whale and human eye, it is important to understand the basic parts and functions of the eye. "Think of the eye like a camera," explained Dr. Jeffery Fasick, assistant professor of biological sciences at Kean University in New Jersey who has researched marine mammal eyes. "You have a lens in front for focusing and a film in the back for recording." The colors we see are determined by our eye's sensitivity to certain wavelengths of light. Photoreceptors called cones allow us to see in bright light, while rods help us see in less light. The human eye contains three types of cones: red, blue, and green-sensitive. The combination of these allows us to see one million hues and colors during the day. We only have one type of rod, though, which is why we can only see in black and white at night. "Whales only have one cone, which is sensitive to green light, so they see in black and white in light and dark," Fasick said. They have one cone and one rod, both of which are sensitive to light in the blue/green range of the color spectrum. "They match their cones and rods to the color of the water. To them, everything is bright," explained Fasick. This means that anything that looks blue or green to the human eye is invisible in the water to whales. The one color that whales can see as a dark shape in their bright, watery environment is red. Copepods, the main food source for right whales, are red, allowing whales to see a group of them as a dark mass. "Sight is the best way to get the most information in a short period of time," said Fasick. "Their eyes bulge out to focus on the mouth to see copepods go in." Dr. Scott Kraus, vice president of research at the New England Aquarium in Boston wanted to know what would happen if fishing gear came in different colors, vertical lines in particular. Kraus wanted to find out if different colors would contrast with the color of the water, allowing whales to see a potential threat. To investigate, Kraus used different colored PVC pipes and placed them in front of whales. This research is still underway, so no conclusions have been made yet. However, Fasick points out that there is very little known about the optics of the eye, so color may not be the only player in the search to understand what whales see. "We (scientists) know almost nothing about the optics of the eye," Fasick said. "We know they have a shallow eye cup, which makes us believe a whale's focal point is only a few meters in the distance. So even if whales can pick out red vertical lines, they would have to be close to them for the gear to be in focus." Fasick also pointed out that we don't know how smart right whales are. "What you have is a large, slow moving whale with poor vision. They might see gear as a foreign object, but they don't know how to react to it," he said. In order to learn more about right whales' eyes, Fasick said researchers need an eye to study. "We had a good eye from a stranded calf, but it still wasn't great," he said. There aren't many right whale strandings and even when there are, the tissues in the eye aren't fresh, explained Fasick. The best option, according to Fasick, is to study bowhead whale eyes. "[They] are a good source of tissue and are kissing cousins of the right whales," he said. In depth studies on other marine mammals' eyesight have revealed interesting findings. Bottlenose dolphins were found to have acute, focused vision both in and out of water. "Bottlenose dolphins are able to focus straight ahead and to their side," said Fasick. Eyesight appears to be important to dolphins for locating food, but they also rely on echolocation, something right whales lack. "We are just starting to embark on the senses," Fasick said. There is still much to be learned about right whales, but Kraus and Fasick have made great strides towards better understanding what whales see. And if Kraus' research finds that right whales can see red rope well enough to swim around it, fishermen may start seeing some colorful changes to their gear. Growing Independence and Fluency Reading with fluency beginning readers to become more successful readers. Reading with one important aspect of fluency. Expressive reading helps book language alive, and written words sound more interesting and natural when read plenty of expression. During this lesson, students will work on their fluency and expression through reading and rereading decodable words in a 1) Which Shoes Do You Theater script (8, each with a single part highlighted) aaronshep.net Script will be modified slightly to make it Popsicle sticks (each labeled with a single character/part) 3) Large sentence strip with "Mom, Brad hit me!" written on it. 4) 8 sentence strips each with one of the following phrases: "Ah! A spider is crawling on my desk!", "Mom, May I have cake lunch? Please?", "Yay! I won the bee!", "I am getting so sleepy.", "Can we go on just ride, please, can we? Pretty please?", "I am starving!", "My big brother is so mean!", & "Stop that! Put that right now! I said, 'Now'!" Peer Evaluation Checklists Does your partner try____ 3rd try____ tone of voice? 1st try____ 3rd try___ tempo when necessary? 1st try___ 2nd try____ 3rd 6) Teacher Evaluation Checklist (Does the student read smoothly? Does the tone of voice? Does tempo when necessary?) a group of 8 students: "Since we are all becoming such wonderful today we're going to do something special. We're going to all become we are going to practice reading a play! You are each going to have part. You will each draw a popsicle stick from this cup. Later in this you will read the part that is written on your popsicle stick." teacher will have popsicle sticks in a cup and instruct students to each draw only one. After the students each assigned their part, the teacher will then have students to lay the sticks on the table in front of them. "Good readers are sometimes kind of like actors. Think of your favorite television show. Would that show be very interesting if all the actors read their parts?" (pause for student response) "Of course not! Let's look at this sentence. (hold up sentence strip) What does it say? students to answer). Right, it says, 'Mom, Brad hit me!' Pretend I'm in backseat of my mom's car, and my little brother just hit me. Would I Brad. Hit. Me." ? (Read aloud choppily with no expression) Did that real? Nope, that didn't sound real at all! Good readers think about happening and pretend that they are actually saying the sentences they read. You wouldn't say, 'Mom. Brad. Hit. would probably say, 'MOOOOOOM, BRAD HIT ME!' Which was more interesting? The way I said it the first time or second? Right! When you read sentences and words the way that you would actually say them, you are reading with expression. Reading with a lot of fun, but we need to reread our lines to be able to get better. actors have to practice their lines many times before they get them and we have to practice reading and rereading so that we will get read with more expression each time!" now that we know that we should read with expression, let me show you how to read with expression." (Pass out each child's script). "Look at the very first sentence on your scripts. I am going to show you how I reading this sentence." Teacher reads slowly, and choppily with little expression: "'Kate. Was. T-teared. Of. Wearing. The. same. Old. shoes.' Oh, tired. She was tired of wearing the same old shoes." To the group of students: I had a little trouble reading word, tired. I accidentally read "teared" instead, but I remembered that what I read had to make sense, so I realized that word was "tired" when I got to the end of my sentence. I remembered to cross-check!" was okay, but I bet I can do even better! I'm going to try it again! reads slowly, with minimal expression): 'Kate was tired of wearing the shoes.' I did do better! That was still a little boring, though. I read all the words the same. I'm going it again, and this time, I'm going to try to read it even faster and plenty of expression-some words might be louder or softer, higher or don't want to sound like a robot! (Teacher reads more quickly and with expression, stressing the words "same" and "old"). was much more interesting! The more times I read and practiced it, the better I got reading my line! I want you to do the exact same thing I did when you lines. The more you practice, the more you will sound like a famous instead of a robot! So when I practice, I'm going to work on reading and smoother, and I won't always keep my voice at the same tone like (Model monotone voice). Instead, I will read some words high and some and also say some words slower than others, just like I would do if I saying the line instead of reading the line. Title: How to Beat All Islands in Poptropica Poptropica is a popular online role-playing game that allows players to explore different islands, complete quests, and solve puzzles. With so many islands to conquer, it can be overwhelming for newcomers or even experienced players. This step-by-step guide will provide you with an in-depth explanation on how to beat all islands in Poptropica while offering key tips and guidelines along the way. ### Step 1: Create Your Character When starting out, create your character by customizing their appearance as per your preference. Choose skin color, hair style, clothing, and other accessories wisely since they represent your avatar throughout the game. Keep in mind that certain items may need to be purchased using credits earned within the game. **Tip:** Avoid wearing dark colors when playing mini-games involving color matching as this might make spotting differences more challenging. ### Step 2: Familiarize Yourself with the Map Once you've created your character, familiarize yourself with the map which serves as your navigation hub. Each island represents a separate mission or storyline. Clicking on any island takes you there directly. **Guideline:** Always start from the first available island (usually Tutorial Island) and proceed chronologically. Completing islands in order provides necessary background information and skills required for later levels. ### Step 3: Read Dialogues Carefully Interact with characters through dialogues; these often contain clues about tasks ahead. Pay attention to what they say and remember important details such as names, places, objects, etc., as they could help you solve upcoming challenges. **Key Tip:** Some conversations offer multiple response options. Choosing correctly sometimes leads to useful rewards or shortcuts, whereas incorrect choices might lead to dead ends or penalties. ### Step 4: Collect Items and Use Them Strategically I see somebody with the shell on its hand. yeah, the moon will come back in a circle. Many places are too cold or too dry for them. Forests can exist from the equator to near the polar regions, but different climates have different kinds of forests. In cold climates conifers dominate, but in temperate zone and tropical climates forests are mainly made up of flowering plants. Different rainfall also makes different kinds of forest. No forests exist in deserts, just a few trees in places where their roots can get at some underground water. See if we can find one to put on there. Another method is to use the aberration of light, discovered and explained by James Bradley in the 18th century. This effect results from the vector addition of the velocity of light arriving from a distant source (such as a star) and the velocity of its observer (see diagram on the right). A moving observer thus sees the light coming from a slightly different direction and consequently sees the source at a position shifted from its original position. Since the direction of the Earth's velocity changes continuously as the Earth orbits the Sun, this effect causes the apparent position of stars to move around. From the angular difference in the position of stars, it is possible to express the speed of light in terms of the Earth's velocity around the Sun. This, with the known length of a year, can be easily converted to the time needed to travel from the Sun to the Earth. In 1729, Bradley used this method to derive that light travelled 10,210 times faster than the Earth in its orbit (the modern figure is 10,066 times faster) or, equivalently, that it would take light 8 minutes 12 seconds to travel from the Sun to the Earth. January is a winter month in the Northern Hemisphere and a summer month in the Southern Hemisphere. In each hemisphere, it is the seasonal equivalent of July in the other. Perihelion, the point in its orbit where the Earth is closest to the Sun, also occurs in this month, between January 2 and January 5. January begins on the same day of the week as October in common years and on the same day of the week as April and July in leap years. January ends on the same day of the week as February and October in common years and on the same day of the week as July in leap years. January is the only month of the year that always has a "twin" - a month that both begins and ends on the same day of the week as it does. In a common year, this is October, and in a leap year, July. The mail goal is to understand what it means to comprehend. This lesson will help teach children how to understand and comprehend a story through summarization. Materials: Paper, pencils, a copy of the book Where the Wild Things Are by Maurice Sendak for each student , a copy of Spider and the Fly(or whatever book the children enjoy) some sort of board that all of the children can see. 1.( Introduce the lesson by explaining to the young reader that understanding what is being read is very and how important it is.) While we read books, we should be able to comprehend enough information so we can give a summary. A summary is when you absorb a lot of information, but you combine that information into a short paragraph. In most cases the summary will contain the main points of the story 2. I will give you guys an example. I am going to read the Spider and the Fly. After reading this book I will give you a summary, but I will need your help in picking out what you guys remembered. A big part of a summary is that it is in order of how the story goes. 3. Did you guys enjoy this story? We will start with the first few pages. And think real hard about the main characters and what they do in the story. What does one character do to another? Good job, boys and 2. I want to see how each of you can write a summary and if you are able to grasp another story individually. To do this, I want each of you to take a copy of the book Where the Wild Things Are. As you read this book be thinking of what the main point of the story is going 3. (I am first going to introduce the book to grab their attention.) Boys and girls Where the Wild Things Are is about a little boy who acts up and does not mind. He wishes to go far, far away he travels days, weeks, and years. He arrives at a mysterious island and sees really big monsters, but you will have to read and find out what they do with him. 3. Since we are finished, I will start you guys out with how to pick out the main points. I will start with the first few pages and I want you to help me pick out what you remember out of those pages that sound important to the story. Remember when you summarize a book you should be able to read your summary to another person to have them understand what the book was about. I would like for you to take out your paper and pencils and write down what you think is the main point of the book. When you finish that, then I want you to write down any other ideas you think are important from the book. 4. I will be collecting these so think very carefully about what you think is important. Remember, we only want the main point and a few other important ideas. 5. (For assessment, the teacher will read each child's summary to see if they have comprehended the main points. Title: How to Create Cute andUniqueHairstyles Creating cute and unique hairstyles can be a fun way to express your personality and creativity. With some practice and patience, you can learn various techniques that will help you achieve stunning results. This comprehensive guide provides detailed steps and helpful tips on creating adorable and distinctive hairstyles suitable for different occasions. ### Section 1: Preparation **Step 1:** Gather Necessary Tools and Supplies Before starting, make sure you have these essential tools and supplies at hand: - Hairbrush or comb - Hair ties (elastic bands) - Bobby pins - Hair clips - Rat tail comb (for sectioning hair) - Curling iron, flat iron, or hot rollers (optional) - Heat protectant spray (if using heated styling tools) - Hair gel, mousse, or hairspray (for hold and control) Having all necessary items ready will ensure smooth execution of the chosen hairstyle without interruptions. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** - Keep your hair clean and tangle-free by brushing or combing it thoroughly before attempting any hairstyle. - Use high-quality styling products specifically designed for your hair type (e.g., fine, thick, curly, straight) to optimize results and minimize damage. - Protect your hair from heat exposure by applying a heat protectant product when using curling irons, flat irons, or hot rollers. ### Section 2: Basic Techniques How about doing it the other way? Japanese has five vowel sounds that can have two different lengths. They are "a, i, u, e, o". In IPA they are transliterated as /a/, /i/, / /, /e/, /o/; and they are pronounced in English as "ah, ee, oo, eh, oh". Lengthening a vowel can change the meaning of the word: "ojisan" ( , uncle) and "ojiisan" ( , grandfather). Japanese has a sound that is like the English "l", but it is also like the English "r". (That is why it can be difficult for many Japanese when to learn to make both sounds when they speak English.) Japanese has a sound that is not uncommon in English and is usually written "Tsu" ( ). This sound appears in "tsunami" ( ), the Japanese word for large ocean waves caused by earthquakes or extreme weather. Do you think it looks like a telephone pole? You got your ch chair pulled in? Rumours about astronauts being able to see the Great Wall from the moon are scientifically not proven. The Great Wall has shown up in radar images taken from space, but scientists are sure it is not possible for astronauts to see the wall with a naked eye. One astronaut who spoke about the visibility of the Great Wall from space was Neil Armstrong. He said that on the moon, it was very clear that the wall was not visible. However, astronaut William Pogue was able to see the wall from a Low Earth Orbit distance (300-530 km height), but only with binoculars and with lots of practice. [brushes Sarah's hair while she continues to feed herself then blinks as if the brush pulls and grunts but continues eating and drinking] [picks up the cup and pretends to drink] Tides of the Ocean Each day the ocean surface rises and falls - a phenomenon called tides. The highest point of the rise in the ocean surface is called high tide. The lowest point is called low tide. Most places have two high tides and two low tides each day. Tides happen because of the gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun on ocean water. Because Earth is rotating, the tides change over time. The Moon has a much greater impact on tides than the Sun because it is much closer. The Moon pulls the water that is closest to it creating a high tide on the side of the Earth closest to the Moon. The ocean water forms a high tide on the opposite side of the Earth too. Low tides occur in areas between the high tides. The difference between high tide and low tide is called the tidal range. Some places have a larger tidal range than others because of differences in the shape of the ocean floor. The difference between high and low tide can be as little as a few centimeters to as much as several meters depending on location. In Canada's Bay of Fundy the tidal range is very large - 16 meters - because of the narrow shape of the bay which funnels the tidewater into a small space. Tides also differ at different times of the year depending on the location of the Moon and Sun in relation to Earth. At times when the Sun, Earth, and Moon are in line with each other (during full and new moons), the tidal range is larger because both the Sun's and Moon's gravitational pull create the tide. This is called a spring tide. At times when the Moon and Sun are not in line with Earth, when they are at right angles when viewed from Earth, the tidal range is smaller. This is called a neap tide. Tides are a powerful force and, because of this, scientists and engineers are working on methods to extract some of that power to produce electricity. This can be done by channeling tidewater to flow through turbines that generate electricity. A tidal power plant located in an estuary in Brittany, France has turbines that turn when the tide is going in and when it is going out. Systems like this may be a danger to wildlife, thus, scientists are studying estuarine ecosystems to understand how it might be affected by turbines. Do another circle 'cause you did such a good job! Physics Lesson: Temperature and the Speed of Sound Temperature is also a condition that affects the speed of sound. Heat, like sound, is a form of kinetic energy. Molecules at higher temperatures have more energy, thus they can vibrate faster. Since the molecules vibrate faster, sound waves can travel more quickly. The speed of sound in room temperature air is 346 meters per second. This is faster than 331 meters per second, which is the speed of sound in air at freezing temperatures. The formula to find the speed of sound in air is as follows: v = 331m/s + 0.6m/s/C * T v is the speed of sound and T is the temperature of the air. One thing to keep in mind is that this formula finds the average speed of sound for any given temperature. The speed of sound is also affected by other factors such as humidity and air pressure. [2w Elijah. Means that that's the train we use to go and visit him] Creating a bundle in Google Inbox can help you organize your emails and keep your inbox clutter-free. A bundle is a group of similar emails that are automatically categorized and moved to a specific section in your inbox. For example, you could create a bundle for all emails related to work, bills, or social media updates. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to create your own bundle in Google Inbox: 1. Open Google Inbox on your computer or mobile device. Make sure you're signed in to your Google account. 2. At the top left corner of the screen, click on the three horizontal lines (also known as the hamburger menu) to open the main navigation panel. 3. Scroll down until you see the "Bundles" option. Click on it. 4. On the Bundles page, you will see a list of pre-made bundles created by Google. These include Social, Promos, Updates, Forums, and Purchases. You can customize these bundles if you want, but for this tutorial, we will focus on creating a new one from scratch. 5. To create a new bundle, click on the blue "+ Create New Bundle" button at the bottom right corner of the screen. 6. Give your new bundle a name that accurately describes its purpose. For example, if you want to collect all emails related to work, you could name it "Work." If you want to gather all bills and receipts, you could call it "Bills & Receipts." The name should be descriptive enough so that when you see it later, you know what kind of emails it contains. 7. Choose whether you want the bundle to appear in your inbox always, only when there are new messages, or never. This depends on your personal preference. If you choose "Always," then every time you check your email, the bundle will show up with any new messages. If you select "Only when there are new messages," then the bundle will only appear when there are unread emails inside it. And if you pick "Never," the bundle won't show up in your inbox, but you can still access it through the Bundles tab. 8. Now comes the most crucial part - setting up the rules for your bundle. Rules determine which emails get added to the bundle based on certain criteria. There are several options to choose from: * From: Enter an email address or domain that sends you the type of emails you want to add to the bundle. For instance, if you want to collect all emails from your boss, enter their email address here. * Has the words: Type keywords or phrases that frequently appear in the subject line or body of the emails you want to collect. For example, if you want to gather all emails about project deadlines, you could put "deadline" or "project due date" here. Cities in the US are usually very-left leaning. And he's got a policeman's uniform, hasn't he? = = = children/CHIDES_NA/NewmanRatner/Interviews/24/7183TB.cha = = = Let's imagine you have a bunch of toys that you need to stack up in a line. You want to make sure that each toy is stable and doesn't fall over. To do this, you tilt the pile of toys slightly so that the center of gravity shifts towards the base, making it more difficult for the toys to topple. This concept is similar to something called "convexity" in mathematics. Now, let's say you have a lot of different ways to stack these toys, each with its own slope or angle. These different stacks are like what mathematicians call "functions." Each function has its own rate of change or "derivative," just like how steeply your tower leans. In our case, we have many different ways to stack the toys (functions), and we know that as we add more toys (as x increases), the stability of the stack changes slowly and smoothly. We also know that if we keep adding toys, eventually the way we stack them will settle down to one specific way of stacking - this is when our function approaches its limit. But here comes the tricky part! What happens if we start changing the way we stack the toys faster and faster? Will the rate at which we change the stack (the derivative) still approach some value? It turns out that yes, under certain conditions, the derivative does indeed approach a limiting value too. In other words, if we take a really, really close look at how fast the stack is leaning as we add more toys, it will get closer and closer to a particular number. The theorem mentioned earlier states that if we have a sequence of toy stacking methods (functions) that converges to a single method (limit function) and those individual stacking methods are "convex and differentiable," then their slopes (derivatives) will also approach the slope of the final stacking method. So not only does the height of the toy tower become more predictable as we add more toys, but even the speed at which the tower grows levels off too! This idea helps us understand calculus better and shows up in various branches of math and science where rates of change matter. However, proving this result requires advanced mathematical tools beyond elementary school curriculum; thus, our simplified explanation focuses on relating complex concepts to real-life situations children can grasp easily. Title: How to Perform a 180 Degree Turn While Skating on Heelys Introduction: Heelys are wheeled shoes that have gained popularity due to their unique design which allows users to skate around effortlessly. One exciting trick you can learn while wearing Heelys is doing a 180-degree turn or spin. This move adds flair to your skating and enhances your skills. Here's our comprehensive guide on performing this cool maneuver safely and effectively. Materials Needed: * A pair of well-fitting Heelys with wheels intact * Protective gear (knee pads, elbow pads, wrist guards, and helmet) * Smooth, flat surface free from obstacles and debris Step 1: Familiarize Yourself With Your Heelys Ensure you feel comfortable wearing your Heelys before attempting any tricks. Walk around in them indoors or outside until they start feeling like an extension of your feet. The more familiar you become with these shoes, the easier it will be to do a 180-degree turn. Key Tip: Make sure your wheels are tightened correctly; loose wheels could cause accidents during complex moves. Step 2: Put On Safety Gear Always wear protective gear when practicing new tricks. It might seem unnecessary if you're just starting out, but as you progress into advanced techniques, injuries may occur without proper protection. Guideline: Don't skip safety measures even if you think you won't fall. Accidents happen unexpectedly, so always stay prepared. Step 3: Find a Suitable Surface Choose a smooth, level area free from traffic, gravel, or other hazards where you can practice your spins comfortably. Avoid wet surfaces because they increase the risk of slipping. Step 4: Position Your Feet Correctly Stand straight with both heels touching the ground ready to push off. Place your dominant foot slightly ahead of the non-dominant one - imagine forming a 'V' shape with your toes pointing away from each other. Step 5: Prepare For Takeoff Bend your knees slightly and lean forward a bit over your front foot. Engage the heel wheel of your back foot by lifting your toe slightly off the ground. Keep your weight centered above your front foot. Step 6: Initiate The Spin Push forcefully against the ground using your back foot. As soon as you gain momentum, lift your back foot entirely off the ground and swing it around in a circular motion towards your front side. Remember to maintain balance over your planted foot throughout the process. Step 7: Landing The Trick During the rotation, transfer your bodyweight onto your now-front (previously rear) foot. Once completed, gently lower your swung-around foot back down, ensuring its heel wheel engages smoothly. You should now face the opposite direction after completing the 180-degree turn. Key Tips: * Practice maintaining balance by standing still on only one wheel at a time. * Start slow, gradually increasing speed once you master control and stability. * Focus on landing softly rather than stomping hard on the ground. Conclusion: With patience, persistence, and consistent practice, executing a clean 180-degree spin on Heelys becomes second nature. Always remember safety first, warm up properly, take breaks whenever needed, and most importantly, enjoy yourself! Let's see what I can build for you to knock down. Elements can join (react) to form pure compounds (such as water, salts, oxides, and organic compounds). In many cases, these compounds have a fixed composition and their own structure and properties. The properties of the compound may be very different from the elements it is made from. Sodium is a metal that burns when put into water and chlorine is a poisonous gas. When they react together they make "sodium chloride" (salt) which is generally harmless in small quantities and edible. And you gave little baby American superhero his lion? Let's talk about comparing infinite sums, which is what the snippet above is all about. You might have heard of adding up numbers to get a total before, like if you have 5 apples and your friend gives you 3 more, you can find out how many apples you have altogether (which is 8) by adding those two numbers together. But what if we want to add up infinitely many numbers, like 1 + 1/2 + 1/4 + 1/8 + 1/16 and so on forever? That's where things get trickier, because we can't just keep adding numbers together endlessly. Instead, we need to use some mathematical tools to figure out whether the sum gets bigger and bigger without any end, or whether it approaches a certain number as we add more and more terms. One way to do this is by comparing the series we're interested in to another series whose behavior we already know. In the snippet above, we compare the series 1/(2^n - 1) to the series 1/2^n, where n goes from 1 to infinity. We call these "sequence terms", since each value depends on which term in the sequence we are looking at. By comparing the limits of these sequences, we can see that they both approach zero as n gets larger and larger. Specifically, the limit of 2^n / (2^n - 1) is 1, which means that the two sequence terms get closer and closer to each other as n grows. Once we establish that our original series behaves similarly to a known convergent series, we can say that our original series also converges. This is similar to saying that if we have two bags of apples, and one bag has fewer apples than the other, then the first bag will always have fewer apples no matter how many more apples we add to either bag. So in short, comparing infinite sums to other series we already know about is a powerful tool for figuring out whether they converge or not. And just like how organizing objects into rows and columns can help us solve puzzles, understanding how to manipulate and compare infinite sums can open up new possibilities for solving complex problems! [Adam had asked for a magnet several sessions before] [moving around and moves the book in MOT's hand] Choosing sewing thread may seem like a simple task, but with so many options available, it can be overwhelming for beginners and even experienced seamstresses. The right type of thread can make your project look professional and last longer, while the wrong one can lead to frustrating results. Here's our comprehensive guide on how to choose sewing thread: **Step 1: Identify Your Fabric Type** The first step is to determine what kind of fabric you will be working with as this will significantly influence your choice of thread. Different fabrics require different types of threads due to their varying weights, textures, and weaves. For instance, delicate fabrics like silk or chiffon need fine, smooth threads to prevent damage and ensure invisibility, whereas heavier materials such as denim or canvas call for stronger, more durable threads. **Step 2: Understand Thread Construction** Sewing threads come in two basic constructions: spun and filament. Spun threads are made from short staple fibers that have been twisted together. They're soft, versatile, and suitable for most general-purpose sewing tasks. Filament threads, however, are composed of continuous strands of synthetic fiber like polyester or nylon. These threads are stronger, smoother, and shinier than spun threads, making them ideal for topstitching, quilting, and sewing heavy-duty fabrics. **Step 3: Match Thread Weight To Fabric Weight** To achieve optimal results, match the weight of your thread to the weight of your fabric. Lightweight fabrics should be paired with lightweight threads (usually marked as 50 or 60 weight), medium-weight fabrics with all-purpose threads (40 or 50 weight), and heavyweight fabrics with heavy-duty threads (30 weight). **Step 4: Choose Appropriate Fiber Content** Thread fibers should complement the fibers used in your fabric. Natural fibers like cotton work well with natural fabrics, while synthetics pair better with manmade materials. This ensures compatibility in terms of strength, flexibility, and appearance. Also consider factors like stretch, resistance to heat, sunlight, chemicals, and moisture when selecting your thread fiber content. **Step 5: Decide On Color** Color selection might seem straightforward, but there are still some things to keep in mind. If you want your stitches to blend in, pick a color close to your fabric's base hue. Contrasting colors create visible lines which can be desirable for decorative purposes or when using multiple layers of similar colored fabrics. Always test your chosen color against your actual fabric under proper lighting conditions before commencing your project. **Key Tips & Guidelines:** * Always buy high-quality threads as they tend to be consistent, strong, and less prone to tangling or breaking during sewing. * Pre-wound bobbins save time and reduce waste. However, hand-winding gives you greater control over tension and prevents birdnesting. * When dealing with fraying edges, zigzag stitching or serging provides reinforcement. Use woolly nylon thread for serger loops as it has excellent elasticity. * Consider using double needle technique for hems on knit fabrics to maintain stretchability without distortion. * Store your threads properly - away from direct sunlight, dust, and humidity - to prolong their lifespan. By following these steps and guidelines, you'll be able to confidently select the perfect sewing thread for any project! 'Cause the dog can't go in the bus.
